content.headline: Salt doesn't just make things taste salty — it chemically suppresses bitterness and amplifies sweetness and savoriness at the same time.
points: Sodium ions block bitter taste receptors, which lets sweet and savory flavors register more clearly
Salt draws moisture out of food through osmosis — useful for drawing out excess water from vegetables, or building flavor in proteins
Salt added early integrates into food structure; salt added at the end sits on the surface and tastes sharper
Diamond Crystal kosher, Morton kosher, and table salt have different crystal sizes and densities — a teaspoon of each is a different amount of salt
content.tell_me_more: The reason a pinch of salt in coffee or chocolate makes it taste richer rather than salty is directly traceable to the bitterness suppression mechanism. Coffee and dark chocolate are both highly bitter; salt's sodium ions reduce the bitterness signal to your brain, and suddenly you taste more of the sweetness and roasted complexity that was there all along. This is also why food that's under-salted seems to taste 'flat' — without salt suppressing the background bitterness, everything registers at the same muted level rather than having distinct peaks.

missions.title: Quick Check
missions.description: Test your understanding of what salt actually does to food and flavor.
missions.why_it_matters: Checking what you understand now makes it stick.

questions.question_text: Why does properly seasoned food not taste salty?
options: Because the salt evaporates during cooking and leaves only flavor compounds behind
Because the right amount of salt amplifies other flavors rather than being perceptible itself
Because cooking breaks down sodium chloride into compounds that don't taste salty
Because salt is diluted by the moisture in the food during cooking
questions.explanation: Salt's flavor-enhancing effect happens below the threshold where salt itself is perceptible. At the right level, it suppresses bitterness and amplifies sweetness and savory notes, making other flavors louder without announcing itself. When food tastes salty, you've crossed the threshold where salt becomes the dominant flavor — the goal is to stay just below it.

questions.question_text: You're making a cucumber salad and want to remove excess water before dressing it. What's the correct technique?
options: Salt the cucumbers and let them sit for 20–30 minutes, then pat dry
Rinse the cucumbers in cold water immediately before dressing
Squeeze the cucumbers by hand to remove the water
Add the dressing immediately and the salt in the dressing will draw out water over time
questions.explanation: Salt draws water out of vegetables through osmosis — the movement of water from an area of lower salt concentration (inside the cucumber) to higher concentration (the salt on the surface). Letting it sit gives osmosis time to work. Rinsing immediately or adding dressing last-minute doesn't give osmosis time to happen, so the cucumber stays waterlogged and dilutes the dressing.

questions.question_text: A recipe calls for 1 teaspoon of kosher salt. You only have table salt. What should you do?
options: Use 1 teaspoon of table salt — they're both sodium chloride, so the amount is the same
Use about half the amount — table salt is much finer and denser than kosher salt
Use twice the amount — table salt is less concentrated than kosher salt
Avoid cooking the recipe — you can't substitute table salt for kosher salt
questions.explanation: Table salt has much smaller crystals than kosher salt, which means a teaspoon of table salt is significantly denser and contains roughly twice as much sodium by volume as Diamond Crystal kosher salt. Using a full teaspoon of table salt where the recipe means kosher salt will over-season the dish noticeably. When in doubt, use about half and taste as you go.

missions.title: Salt at Different Stages
missions.description: Reflect on a time when you added salt to something at different points and noticed how the flavor changed.
missions.why_it_matters: Understanding the mechanism of salt is useful; experiencing it is what makes it automatic.
missions.prompt: Have you ever tasted a dish that was clearly under-seasoned — where adding salt at the table just made it taste salty rather than fixing it? Or have you noticed the difference between food seasoned during cooking versus at the end? Describe what the difference was, and based on what you learned in this lesson, why you think that happened.
